skill.name: code-review
skill.description: Automates pull request review with specialized agents for guideline compliance, bug detection, and logic analysis.
skill.summary: Runs multi-agent pull request reviews and can post high-confidence inline feedback.
skill.author: Bind

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many weak cryptography and command patterns, but the cryptography matches are false positives in prose and Markdown examples. The confirmed risk is purpose-aligned command execution through unrestricted Bash, GitHub CLI operations, Bun scripts, and optional PR comment posting.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted Bash Permission
medium_findings.description: The slash command declares Bash(*) as an allowed tool. This permits broad shell execution during review workflows, so users must trust both the skill instructions and invoked repository tooling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicit and broad. The risk is elevated, but it supports the stated code review workflow rather than showing malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Repository Tool Execution During Review
medium_findings.description: The workflow instructs the assistant to run Bun scripts and GitHub CLI commands. These commands are legitimate for PR review, but they execute local tooling and may expose repository data through authenticated GitHub calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly documented and require authenticated GitHub access. No evidence shows exfiltration beyond normal PR review behavior.

medium_findings.title: Optional External Write Action
medium_findings.description: When the comment option is used, the workflow can post inline comments to a GitHub pull request. This is expected functionality, but it creates an external side effect on a shared code review surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write action is clearly described and gated by user confirmation. It is not malicious, but marketplace users should understand the publication risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Detections
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography matches appear in descriptions, Markdown formatting, review terminology, and code link guidance. No cryptographic API use, hashing implementation, or weak algorithm selection was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found prose and Markdown context at the cited locations. There is no evidence of cryptographic code or security-sensitive algorithm use.

low_findings.title: False Positive Hardcoded URL Detections
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are a GitHub CLI documentation link and a GitHub code link format example. They are not endpoints used for hidden network requests or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and formatting examples. I did not find evidence of covert HTTP calls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Shell Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill permits Bash(*) and directs execution of review helper commands. This should be disclosed because shell execution can have filesystem, network, and credential side effects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The broad Bash permission and command usage are explicit. The pattern is risky by capability, not by confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated GitHub Side Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow can use authenticated GitHub tooling to inspect pull requests and post comments. This is useful, but it can publish AI-generated feedback to a remote service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly documents the post-comment path. User confirmation reduces severity, but the side effect remains real.

content.user_title: Review pull requests with AI agents
content.value_statement: Pull request reviews are slow when teams must check guidelines, bugs, and logic by hand. This skill coordinates focused agents to produce high-confidence review feedback for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether a pull request should be reviewed before continuing.
Finds relevant AGENTS.md guideline files for changed paths.
Summarizes pull request title, description, changed files, and change intent.
Runs parallel review agents for compliance, bug detection, and logic analysis.
Validates candidate findings and filters out low-confidence issues.
Can post confirmed inline comments when the comment option is used.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication and supporting github-pr helper scripts.
Uses shell commands, so users should run it only in trusted repositories.
Focuses on high-confidence issues and may miss subtle design concerns.
Posting comments can affect live pull requests and should be reviewed first.

use_cases.title: Pre-merge quality check
use_cases.description: Run a structured review before merging a pull request into a shared branch.

use_cases.title: Guideline compliance review
use_cases.description: Check whether a pull request follows applicable AGENTS.md rules for changed files.

use_cases.title: Review assistance for contributors
use_cases.description: Get focused feedback before requesting a human review from a teammate.

prompt_templates.title: Run a terminal review
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /code-review on the current pull request and show the findings in the terminal only.

prompt_templates.title: Review with guideline focus
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /code-review and pay close attention to AGENTS.md compliance for files changed in this pull request.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare inline comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /code-review --comment, summarize the proposed comments, and ask before posting anything to GitHub.

prompt_templates.title: High-signal regression review
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /code-review and report only validated issues that can cause incorrect behavior, security impact, or clear guideline violations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review for noisy or complex changes

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that changes authentication middleware.
output_examples.output: The skill summarizes the PR, checks applicable guidelines, and reports validated issues with file locations and confidence scores.

output_examples.input: Run the review with the comment option enabled.
output_examples.output: The skill presents a summary of proposed inline comments and asks for confirmation before posting them.

output_examples.input: Review a draft or already-reviewed pull request.
output_examples.output: The skill stops early when the pre-check indicates that review is not needed.

best_practices: Run the skill from a clean, trusted repository checkout.
Review proposed inline comments before approving publication.
Keep AGENTS.md instructions clear, local to changed files, and current.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill in untrusted repositories with unknown helper scripts.
Do not post comments automatically without reading the proposed feedback.
Do not use it as the only security review for sensitive changes.

faq.question: Does this skill post comments automatically?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow asks for confirmation before posting inline comments when the comment option is used.

faq.question: What tools does it require?
faq.answer: It requires GitHub CLI authentication and the bundled github-pr helper skill scripts.

faq.question: Can it review draft pull requests?
faq.answer: The workflow checks for draft, closed, trivial, or already-reviewed pull requests and stops when review is not needed.

faq.question: What kinds of issues does it prioritize?
faq.answer: It prioritizes clear bugs, logic errors, security issues, resource leaks, and explicit AGENTS.md violations.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses broad Bash access and authenticated GitHub actions, which are useful but require user trust and review.

faq.question: Does it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It helps find high-confidence issues, but humans should still review context, product intent, and final decisions.
